jokes.text: Yeah, I probably won't be able to get the parts I need for two, three weeks. And that's if I order them today. Which I won't.
jokes.setup: Customer expects plumber to order parts today to fix the flooding basement
jokes.punchline: Casual admission he won't even order them today
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of contractor indifference. Economy of words makes the punchline land hard.

jokes.text: Emergency call. Gotta go. What should we do until you get back? Put a pan down there.
jokes.setup: Major flooding emergency requires professional solution
jokes.punchline: Professional advice is to put a pan down
jokes.explanation: Classic contractor indifference joke. Simple but effective character comedy.

jokes.text: It didn't work.
jokes.punchline: Obvious failure delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to previous joke. Homer's deadpan delivery of the obvious.

jokes.text: It's a secret. Shut up.
jokes.setup: Homer asks innocent question about parking
jokes.punchline: Aggressive secrecy about mundane topic
jokes.explanation: Sets up the running gag that will pay off throughout the episode. Simple but effective setup.

jokes.text: All right, Econo-Save, you just made the list.
jokes.setup: Homer frustrated by coworkers' mysterious privileges
jokes.punchline: Threatens grocery store for unrelated reason
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - misdirected anger at completely unrelated target. Character-specific humor.

jokes.text: It's a secret. Shut up.
jokes.punchline: Exact same response as before
jokes.explanation: Running gag strengthens. Repetition builds the mystery and Homer's frustration.

jokes.text: It's a secret. Shut up.
jokes.punchline: Third iteration of the same response
jokes.explanation: Rule of three completes the running gag setup. Each repetition increases the comedy.

jokes.text: I do now.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests conspiracy theory about Kennedy assassination
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately adopts the theory
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. Homer's gullibility in two words. Maximum comedy per word.

jokes.text: You stalked Charles Kuralt because you thought he dug up your garden.
jokes.punchline: Specific absurd past stalking incident
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific reference. Charles Kuralt as stalking target is unexpectedly perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, okay. Have it your own way, Marge. I'll be back in a minute. I'm going outside... to stalk... Lenny and Carl...
jokes.punchline: Immediately announces stalking plan with dramatic pauses
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer defiance. The dramatic pauses make it absurdly theatrical.

jokes.text: All I have to do is follow the yellow-drip road.
jokes.setup: Following oil drips from Lenny and Carl's car
jokes.punchline: Wizard of Oz reference adapted to oil leak
jokes.explanation: Solid pop culture reference with good wordplay. Not groundbreaking but well-executed.

jokes.text: I can see everything and they're none the wiser.
jokes.punchline: Obviously visible to everyone (implied visual)
jokes.explanation: Standard incompetent spy trope. Works for Homer's character but not particularly original.

jokes.text: An intruder! He will pay the ultimate price. Yes, the ultimate price. Get out of here!
jokes.punchline: Ultimate price is just being told to leave
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection. Builds up dramatic consequences then deflates completely.

jokes.text: I saw weird stuff in that place last night. Weird, strange, sick, twisted, eerie, godless, evil stuff. And I want in.
jokes.punchline: Despite all the negatives, wants to join
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic and character moment. The escalating adjectives build to perfect punchline.

jokes.text: That was a real nice secret organization we had once.
jokes.punchline: Casually mourns the lost secrecy
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan reaction. The past tense 'had' sells the joke.

jokes.text: Hey! I saved your life. That egg sandwich could've killed you by cholesterol.
jokes.setup: Need to save a Stonecutter's life to join
jokes.punchline: Claims egg sandwich salvation
jokes.explanation: Homer's desperate logic at work. Sets up the next exchange about eggs.

jokes.text: Those Egg Council creeps got to you too?
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert explains egg cholesterol science
jokes.punchline: Homer sees conspiracy by 'Egg Council'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satirical moment. 'Egg Council creeps' is perfect paranoid phrasing.

jokes.text: You'd better run, egg!
jokes.punchline: Threatens an actual egg
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the egg paranoia. Four words of pure Homer absurdity.

jokes.text: Sorry. Not you, Homer. Why not? But you let in Homer Glumpet. It says 'no Homers.' We're allowed to have one.
jokes.punchline: Rule is 'no Homers' but they already have one Homer
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons logic. Absurd technicality that makes perfect sense within its own rules.

jokes.text: We can? Thanks, Mom.
jokes.setup: Marge says kids can be cruel
jokes.punchline: Bart takes it as permission/encouragement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment. Misinterprets statement as encouragement for mischief.

jokes.text: What do they do? What don't they do? Oh, they do so many things, they never stop. Oh, the things they do there. My stars.
jokes.punchline: Elaborate non-answer that says nothing
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa rambling non-answer. Character-specific humor but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Not as such, no.
jokes.setup: Accused of not knowing what Stonecutters do
jokes.punchline: Admits complete ignorance in formal language
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation after the elaborate buildup. 'Not as such' is perfectly formal.

jokes.text: Do I ever. It's five years later and I still think I'm a chicken. I'm a chicken, Marge.
jokes.punchline: Still under hypnotic suggestion years later
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd callback to an off-screen event. Homer's earnest confusion sells it.

jokes.text: I'm an Elk, a Mason, a Communist.
jokes.punchline: Casual inclusion of Communist among lodge memberships
jokes.explanation: Perfect casual delivery of incongruous political membership among benign organizations.

jokes.text: I'm the president of the Gay & Lesbian Alliance, for some reason.
jokes.punchline: Unexplained presidency of LGBT organization
jokes.explanation: Absurd membership for Grampa's character. 'For some reason' sells the confusion.

jokes.text: What kind of stupid wiener name is that? 'Hello, my name is Number One.' And so forth.
jokes.punchline: Mocks the name with imagined introduction
jokes.explanation: Homer's crude mockery of formal titles. 'And so forth' adds nice pompous touch.

jokes.text: Hey, 29, get over here! Thank you, sir. May I have another?
jokes.punchline: Burns masochistically asks for more abuse
jokes.explanation: Good character reversal. Powerful Burns submitting to blue-collar Lenny.

jokes.text: Patience, Monty. Climb the ladder.
jokes.punchline: Lenny condescendingly advises Burns to be patient
jokes.explanation: Nice character reversal continues. Lenny's patronizing tone toward his boss.

jokes.text: I think I have to do it again. My blindfold came off.
jokes.punchline: Wants to repeat deadly stunt due to technicality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer earnestness about following rules, even deadly ones. Great character moment.

jokes.text: Hey, have you ever noticed that the Crossing the Desert is a lot like the Unblinking Eye? And exactly like the Wreck of the Hesperus.
jokes.setup: Different ritual names for same activity
jokes.punchline: Homer notices they're all identical
jokes.explanation: Good satirical observation about meaningless ritual variations. Homer accidentally insightful.

jokes.text: He should have to take a different oath. Everyone takes the same oath.
jokes.setup: Homer's oath about head being plucked of all but three hairs
jokes.punchline: Homer objects since he's already bald
jokes.explanation: Good observation about oath irrelevance to Homer's baldness. Bureaucratic inflexibility.

jokes.text: Now let's all get drunk and play Ping-Pong.
jokes.setup: Solemn ceremony about ancient wisdom and truth
jokes.punchline: Immediately transitions to drinking and games
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of pompous ceremony. Instant transition from sacred to mundane.

jokes.text: But what if I were to shake your hand in this way?
jokes.setup: Plumber still won't fix pipes quickly
jokes.punchline: Secret handshake immediately changes everything
jokes.explanation: Good callback to opening plumber problem. Shows immediate benefits of membership.

jokes.text: So long, suckers.
jokes.punchline: Gloats while gliding past on rollerblades
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer smugness moment. Visual of rollerblades adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: This keeps paramedics from stealing your wallet.
jokes.punchline: Protection from paramedic theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and specific concern about emergency responders. Perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: We just created the greatest democracy on earth, lowlife commoner! Get out!
jokes.setup: Founding Fathers destroying tavern in celebration
jokes.punchline: Celebrates democracy while being elitist and destructive
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical point about founding contradictions. Democracy celebrated through elitism.

jokes.text: These people look deep within my soul and assign me a number based on the order in which I joined.
jokes.punchline: Reveals numbering system is completely arbitrary
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satirical observation about arbitrary organizational hierarchy. Homer's earnestness makes it perfect.

jokes.text: Who controls the British crown? Who keeps the metric system down? We do, We do
jokes.punchline: Bizarre mix of major and minor conspiracies
jokes.explanation: Iconic conspiracy song with perfect escalation of absurd claims. Highly quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Who leaves Atlantis off the maps? Who keeps the Martians under wraps? We do, We do
jokes.punchline: Mix of mythical and sci-fi conspiracies
jokes.explanation: Continues the brilliant conspiracy song with escalating absurdity. Atlantis and Martians perfect.

jokes.text: Who makes Steve Guttenberg a star? We do, We do
jokes.punchline: Most mundane conspiracy: making mediocre actor famous
jokes.explanation: Peak of the conspiracy song. Steve Guttenberg is perfectly random and specific. Became cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Homer. Oh, yeah. Thanks, Lenny.
jokes.punchline: Homer uses it as napkin without thinking
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness. Sets up the major plot turn but understated.

jokes.text: Oh, God! No, Homer, no! This is outrageous! You can't do that! No!
jokes.punchline: Extreme horror at napkin use
jokes.explanation: Good reaction to Homer's oblivious sacrilege. Over-the-top horror sells the joke.

jokes.text: And as a final humiliation, you must walk home naked, dragging behind you the Stone of Shame.
jokes.punchline: Elaborate, specific humiliation ritual
jokes.explanation: 'Stone of Shame' is perfectly pompous and absurd. Sets up the birthmark reveal.

jokes.text: The mark! Oh, that. It's just a birthmark. And I'll thank you not to stare.
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal and social awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response to destiny. Deflates cosmic importance with social embarrassment.

jokes.text: Remove the Stone of Shame. Attach the Stone of Triumph!
jokes.punchline: Ceremonial stone swapping
jokes.explanation: Good callback and escalation. 'Stone of Triumph' perfectly parallels 'Stone of Shame.'

jokes.text: Mental note: Don't overdo this.
jokes.punchline: Self-aware note about restraint
jokes.explanation: Rare Homer self-awareness moment. Ironic since he will definitely overdo it.

jokes.text: I always wondered if there was a God. And now I know. There is, and it's me.
jokes.setup: Homer's power going to his head
jokes.punchline: Declares himself God
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer megalomania. Casual declaration of divinity is character-perfect.

jokes.text: I always wondered if there was a God. And now I know. There is, and it's me.
jokes.setup: Homer enjoying his new godlike status
jokes.punchline: Concludes he himself is God
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Homer's ego. The logical progression from wondering to certainty is brilliant.

jokes.text: Remove the girl.
jokes.setup: Lisa giving Homer wisdom about fleeting glory
jokes.punchline: Dismisses her like a Roman emperor
jokes.explanation: Three words capture Homer's corruption perfectly. Imperial dismissal of family wisdom.

jokes.text: Yeah. Well... Run.
jokes.setup: Homer asks if he's not perfect
jokes.punchline: Honest answer followed by immediate retreat
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from honesty to panic. Moe's practical fear response.

jokes.text: I could help others. I'll get a bunch of monkeys, dress them up and make them reenact the Civil War.
jokes.punchline: Homer's bizarre interpretation of helping people
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd misunderstanding of community service. Perfectly random and specific to Homer.

jokes.text: I could help others. I'll get a bunch of monkeys, dress them up and make them reenact the Civil War.
jokes.punchline: Homer's bizarre interpretation of community service
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - completely missing the point of helping others. The specificity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Dad, that doesn't help people. Couldn't hurt. Unless the monkeys started hurting people. Which they almost certainly would.
jokes.setup: Lisa objects to monkey Civil War plan
jokes.punchline: Homer realizes monkeys would definitely attack people
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the monkey idea. Homer's dawning realization is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: He's gone mad with power, like that Albert Schweitzer guy.
jokes.setup: Homer using power for good deeds
jokes.punchline: Compares to humanitarian doctor as example of power madness
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd reference. Albert Schweitzer as example of dangerous power corruption is perfect.

jokes.text: This is much better than the old Stonecutter hall. Silence. I now call to order the first meeting of the ancient, mystic society of... No Homers.
jokes.punchline: Calls back to childhood treehouse club rules
jokes.explanation: Excellent long-distance callback to the treehouse scene. Adult version of childhood exclusion.

jokes.text: Hey, fellas. Can I join? Sorry. No Homers.
jokes.punchline: Immediately excluded by same rule as childhood
jokes.explanation: Perfect completion of the callback. Homer's eternal exclusion comes full circle.

jokes.text: Homer, you can't just keep hanging out with these Colobus monkeys. Somebody's gonna get parasites.
jokes.punchline: Has actually acquired Civil War monkeys as companions
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the monkey Civil War idea. Specific mention of 'Colobus' adds detail.

jokes.text: Oh, Marge, kids, I miss my club. Oh, Homie. You know, you are a member of a very exclusive club.
jokes.setup: Homer sad about losing club membership
jokes.punchline: Marge suggests family as exclusive club
jokes.explanation: Standard heartwarming setup. Works more as emotional beat than pure comedy.

jokes.text: The Black Panthers?
jokes.setup: Marge mentions Homer belonging to exclusive club
jokes.punchline: Homer's first guess is radical political organization
jokes.explanation: Good Homer non-sequitur. Black Panthers as first thought shows his scattered mind.

jokes.text: And only two of those members have special rings. I meant our wedding rings!
jokes.setup: Marge mentions special rings in family club
jokes.punchline: Has to clarify she means marriage, not secret society
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about Homer's secret society obsession extending to marriage interpretation.

jokes.text: Who controls the British crown? Who keeps the metric system down? We do, We do
jokes.punchline: Adopts conspiracy song for family bonding
jokes.explanation: Sweet callback that transforms the conspiracy song into family bonding moment.

jokes.text: Shut up.
jokes.punchline: Homer uses Lenny and Carl's dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Final callback to the running gag that started the episode. Completes the circle.
